sections.content:

You are still on the same level.

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Clicking on purple rect had no effect. Diamond is back at center. I've been stuck exploring without progress. Let me try a different approach - maybe the diamond needs to be moved to overlap with specific objects to paint them. Let me try RIGHT twice to move it further up-right and see the full range of motion.
- Plan: Try arrow keys and SPACE to discover movement/interaction mechanics.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 29 of about 55 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK so after 2x RIGHT, the diamond is at [[23, 39], [31, 47], [35, 43], [35, 42], [28, 35], [27, 35]] - this is further up-right.
After 2x LEFT, the diamond is at [[23, 24], [27, 28], [28, 28], [35, 21], [35, 20], [31, 16]] - this is further down-left.
So RIGHT and LEFT are NOT inverses! RIGHT moves up-right and LEFT moves down-left, but they don't cancel out perfectly. After RIGHT+LEFT, I end up at a different position than the start.
Wait, let me check. The original position was [[25, 26], [25, 37], [31, 37], [31, 26]]. After RIGHT, it went to [[23, 39], [31, 47], [35, 43], [35, 42], [28, 35], [27, 35]]. After LEFT from there, it should go back... but I did RIGHT, RIGHT, LEFT, LEFT.
After 2x RIGHT: [[23, 39], [31, 47], [35, 43], [35, 42], [28, 35], [27, 35]]
After 1x LEFT from there: would go to somewhere between center and the 2x RIGHT position
After 2x LEFT from there: [[23, 24], [27, 28], [28, 28], [35, 21], [35, 20], [31, 16]]
So the diamond is now at the left position, not the center. I need one more RIGHT to get back to center.
Actually wait, I realize I've been wasting a lot of actions. Let me think about this more carefully.
The diamond moves diagonally. RIGHT moves it up-right, LEFT moves it down-left. The diamond can be at multiple positions along this diagonal.
The rectangles are at fixed positions:
- Purple rect: rows 34-38, cols 27-36
- White rect: rows 39-43, cols 27-36
The diamond's positions:
- Center: rows 25-31, cols 26-37
- Left (1x LEFT from center): rows 23-35, cols 16-28
- Right (1x RIGHT from center): rows 23-35, cols 35-47 (approximately)
The diamond at the left position (rows 23-35, cols 16-28) overlaps with the purple rect (rows 34-38, cols 27-36) at the bottom-right corner. The diamond's bottom is at row 35 and the purple rect's top is at row 34. The diamond's right is at col 28 and the purple rect's left is at col 27. So they're adjacent or slightly overlapping.
But nothing happened when I moved the diamond there. So maybe overlapping doesn't trigger anything.
Let me think about this completely differently. What if the goal is to arrange the diamond and rectangles in a specific configuration? Or what if the goal is to change the colors of the rectangles to match the template?
Actually, I just realized something. The template shows white above purple. The center objects are purple diamond, purple rect, white rect. What if I need to:
1. Change the diamond to white (click white square)
2. Move the diamond to overlap with the purple rect, painting it white
3. Then the purple rect becomes white, and I have white diamond, white rect, white rect
4. Then change the diamond to purple and move it to overlap with the bottom white rect, painting it purple
5. Result: purple diamond, white rect, purple rect
But that's white above purple, which matches the template!
But I don't know if the diamond paints things it overlaps with. Let me test this hypothesis.
Actually, I've been going in circles. Let me try a completely different approach. What if the goal is to click on the white rect in the template (top-left) to "select" it, then click on the purple rect in the center to paint it white?
Or what if I need to click on the gray background to do something?
Let me try clicking on the white rect in the template.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: Diamond moves diagonally with RIGHT/LEFT but doesn't paint overlapping objects. I've wasted many actions exploring. Let me try clicking on the template objects in the top-left to see if they do something.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# First, let me get the diamond back to center
result = action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After RIGHT (back to center?):")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] > 20 and n['color'] in ['W', 'p']:
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Now try clicking on the white rect in the template (top-left)
print("\nClicking on white template rect at row 5, col 7...")
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 5, 'col': 7}])
print("Result:", last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed'), last_action_result.get('no_impact'))

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After click on template white rect:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] > 20 and n['color'] in ['W', 'p']:
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
